h1Title: Senile Osteoporosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Senile osteoporosis is the age-related form of low bone mass and microarchitectural deterioration that affects adults over 70, driven by long-standing calcium and vitamin D deficiency, reduced osteoblast activity, and secondary hyperparathyroidism rather than the rapid postmenopausal estrogen loss seen in younger women. Roughly 25-30% of women and 6-10% of men over age 75 in the United States meet WHO densitometric criteria for osteoporosis (T-score at or below -2.5 at hip or spine), with one in two women and one in five men over 50 expected to sustain a fragility fracture in their remaining lifetime. Patients usually have no symptoms until they fracture a vertebra, hip, or wrist after minimal trauma. Hip fracture mortality remains 20-30% at one year. Effective oral and intravenous treatments cut hip fracture risk by 30-50% even after age 80.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 25-30% of women and 6-10% of men age 75+ in the United States meet WHO osteoporosis criteria (NHANES 2017-2018)
keyStats.demographics: Women outnumber men 3-4:1; white and Asian elders carry higher risk than Black or Hispanic elders; nursing-home residents three times more affected than community dwellers
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosed predominantly age 70+; hip fracture peak age 80-85
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 200 million people worldwide live with osteoporosis; 8.9 million fragility fractures annually (IOF 2024)

definition: Senile osteoporosis (ICD-10: M81.0 in age-related variant), also called Type II osteoporosis, is a systemic skeletal disorder of adults over 70 characterized by decreased bone mass, deterioration of trabecular and cortical microarchitecture, and increased fracture risk. Unlike Type I postmenopausal osteoporosis, which preferentially erodes trabecular bone in the first 5-10 years after menopause, senile osteoporosis affects both cortical and trabecular bone, develops slowly over decades, and reflects cumulative defects in calcium absorption, renal vitamin D 1-alpha-hydroxylation, sex-hormone production in both sexes, growth-hormone and IGF-1 signaling, and osteoblast number and function. Secondary hyperparathyroidism driven by reduced serum 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D and falling dietary calcium accelerates bone resorption. The condition affects both axial and appendicular skeleton, producing the classic triad of femoral neck, vertebral, and distal radius fractures. The WHO densitometric definition is a hip or spine T-score at or below -2.5 on dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA), but the modern clinical definition also captures any adult age 70+ who sustains a fragility fracture (vertebral, hip, humerus, pelvis) regardless of T-score. Senile osteoporosis is managed by geriatricians, endocrinologists, rheumatologists, orthopedic surgeons, and primary-care physicians, often coordinated through a fracture-liaison service.

typesClassification.description: Adults over 70 of both sexes. Cortical and trabecular bone loss driven by calcium-vitamin D-PTH axis dysfunction, sarcopenia, and reduced osteoblast activity. Hip and pelvic fractures predominate.
Women age 70+ retain residual Type I trabecular bone loss from the menopausal transition layered on senile losses. Vertebral compression and distal radius fractures remain common.

Caused or accelerated by medications (chronic glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, proton-pump inhibitors, SSRIs), endocrine disease (primary hyperparathyroidism, hyperthyroidism, hypogonadism), gastrointestinal malabsorption, renal disease, or malignancy. Identified by targeted secondary-cause work-up.
T-score at or below -2.5 plus one or more fragility fractures. Carries the highest near-term refracture risk (15-20% within 2 years) and warrants aggressive treatment including anabolic agents.

primarySymptoms: No symptoms until fracture occurs in 60-70% of patients; the disease is therefore called a silent epidemic and is diagnosed by densitometry or after a first fragility fracture.

Sudden severe mid-thoracic or lumbar back pain after bending, lifting, or even coughing, indicating a new vertebral compression fracture; pain typically peaks for 4-6 weeks then settles into chronic ache.
Loss of height of 2 cm or more over a year, or cumulative loss of 4 cm or more from young-adult height, signals multiple vertebral fractures.
Progressive thoracic kyphosis (dowager's hump) with forward head posture, restrictive chest wall changes, and a protuberant abdomen as anterior vertebral bodies collapse.
Hip pain, groin pain, or inability to bear weight after a fall from standing height — most commonly femoral neck or intertrochanteric fracture.
Wrist pain and deformity (dinner-fork) after a fall on an outstretched hand — distal radius (Colles) fracture is often the first sentinel event in late middle age.
Chronic mid-back ache, fatigue, and reduced exercise tolerance from kyphosis-related restrictive lung disease and altered abdominal mechanics.
Tooth loss and mandibular bone loss in advanced disease, often noticed by dentists before formal diagnosis.
Reduced grip strength and slow gait speed reflect coexisting sarcopenia, which predicts future fracture independent of bone density.
earlyWarningSigns: Loss of 2 cm or more in standing height during routine annual measurements after age 65
Self-reported difficulty rising from a chair without using arms — a marker of coexisting sarcopenia and falls risk
A first wrist or rib fracture in late middle age after minor trauma; ~50% of patients with a fragility fracture have undiagnosed osteoporosis
Chronic mid-thoracic ache that worsens with prolonged standing and improves with lying down
Family history of maternal hip fracture and personal use of medications that lower bone mass (glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, PPIs, SSRIs)
emergencySigns: Inability to bear weight on a leg after a fall — assume hip fracture and seek emergency assessment within hours
Sudden onset of severe back pain with new lower-limb weakness, numbness, or bowel and bladder dysfunction — suspect vertebral fracture with spinal cord or cauda equina compromise
Acute chest wall pain with shortness of breath after minimal trauma — exclude rib fracture with pneumothorax or hemothorax
New severe back pain with fever, night sweats, or weight loss in an older adult — exclude vertebral osteomyelitis or metastatic fracture mimicking osteoporotic collapse
Sudden cognitive change, hypotension, or significant hemoglobin drop after a femoral neck fracture — covert hemorrhage and delirium often complicate hip fracture in elders

causes.cause: Age-related decline in osteoblast activity and bone formation
causes.description: Mesenchymal stem cells in the marrow shift toward adipogenic rather than osteoblastic lineage with age, while existing osteoblasts produce less type I collagen matrix per cell. The result is a slowly negative bone-remodeling balance: every remodeling cycle removes more bone than it replaces, accelerating after age 70.

causes.cause: Vitamin D deficiency and reduced renal 1-alpha-hydroxylation
causes.description: Skin synthesis of vitamin D3 falls 60-70% by age 75, sun exposure declines, dietary intake is poor, and aging kidneys generate less active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. Resulting low serum calcium triggers secondary hyperparathyroidism. Roughly 50% of community-dwelling adults over 70 have serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D below 20 ng/mL.

Chronically low ionized calcium and high serum phosphate drive parathyroid gland hyperplasia. Sustained elevated PTH increases osteoclast number and lifespan, accelerating cortical bone loss in the femoral neck, hip, and forearm — the sites that fracture in senile disease.

Age-related decline in active vitamin-D-dependent calcium absorption in the duodenum (independent of low vitamin D) reduces calcium retention. Concomitant atrophic gastritis, proton-pump-inhibitor use, and reduced dietary intake from poor appetite and lactose intolerance compound the deficit.

Postmenopausal estrogen depletion (women) and gradual age-related testosterone decline (men) remove their inhibitory effect on osteoclasts. Estrogen also has direct osteoblastic and anti-apoptotic actions. Aromatase activity in fat tissue partially compensates, but reduced muscle and fat mass with aging weakens that buffer.

causes.cause: Long-term glucocorticoid exposure and other medications
causes.description: Chronic oral prednisone above 5 mg/day for more than 3 months suppresses osteoblasts, increases osteoclast survival, and reduces intestinal calcium absorption. Aromatase inhibitors, androgen-deprivation therapy, long-term proton-pump inhibitors, SSRIs, thiazolidinediones, and high-dose loop diuretics also contribute.

causes.cause: Coexisting sarcopenia and reduced mechanical loading
causes.description: Loss of skeletal muscle mass and force after age 70 reduces the strain signal that maintains bone formation. Bed rest, frailty, and sedentary lifestyles further down-regulate Wnt-signaling-driven osteocyte activity. Sarcopenia and osteoporosis together drive osteosarcopenia, the high-risk phenotype for falls and fractures.

riskFactors.description: Each additional decade after 50 doubles hip fracture risk independently of bone density. By age 85, lifetime hip fracture incidence reaches 30% in women and 13% in men (US data).
Women lose 30-50% of trabecular bone and 25-30% of cortical bone over a lifetime; men lose 20-30% and 15-20% respectively. The lifetime fracture risk in women is roughly 2-3 times higher.
A previous fracture after minimal trauma raises near-term refracture risk by 2-5x. The risk peaks in the first 1-2 years after the index fracture (imminent fracture risk).
First-degree maternal hip fracture roughly doubles personal risk independent of measured BMD. Polygenic-risk-score studies confirm a heritability of 50-70% for bone density.
Prednisone 5 mg/day or higher for 3+ months raises fracture risk roughly 2-fold even before BMD declines. Risk persists with cumulative dose and partially reverses on cessation.

Thin elders carry less mechanical load, have less peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogens, and have less padding to absorb fall impact. BMI under 20 raises hip fracture risk roughly 2-fold.
Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D below 20 ng/mL and dietary calcium under 600 mg/day are both associated with measurable BMD decline and fracture excess in adults over 70.

Roughly 95% of hip fractures and 50% of vertebral fractures result from a fall. Risk factors for falls — polypharmacy, sedating drugs, postural hypotension, peripheral neuropathy, poor vision, and home hazards — are themselves fracture risk factors.
Current smoking reduces BMD 5-10% and raises hip fracture risk 1.5-2x. Alcohol intake above 3 units/day similarly raises fracture risk, both via direct osteoblast suppression and via falls.

affectedDemographics: Women age 75+: 25-40% prevalence in NHANES 2017-2018; white and Asian women most affected, Black women retain higher peak bone mass and lower lifetime fracture risk.
Men age 75+: 6-10% prevalence; under-screened and under-treated despite mortality after hip fracture being substantially higher (30-40% at one year) than in women.
Nursing-home residents: prevalence approaches 80% with annual hip-fracture incidence around 4%, three times community-dwelling rates.
Northern-latitude populations (Scandinavia, northern Europe, North America): higher prevalence linked to limited sun exposure and lower 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels.
Asian-American elders: high vertebral but lower hip fracture risk; population-specific T-score cutoffs are debated.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical risk assessment with bone densitometry. The starting point in adults age 70+ is a fracture history and a FRAX or Garvan calculator to estimate 10-year major osteoporotic and hip fracture probabilities. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) of the lumbar spine, femoral neck, and total hip remains the WHO standard: a T-score at or below -2.5 at any site defines osteoporosis, between -1.0 and -2.5 is osteopenia, and -1.0 or above is normal. A T-score above -2.5 plus a fragility fracture also qualifies as clinical osteoporosis and mandates treatment. Vertebral fracture assessment (VFA) by DEXA or thoracolumbar lateral radiograph identifies asymptomatic vertebral fractures, which are present in roughly 15-25% of patients age 70+ and not always reported as back pain. Laboratory work-up rules out secondary causes: complete blood count, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, 25-hydroxyvitamin D, parathyroid hormone, thyroid-stimulating hormone, creatinine and estimated GFR, serum protein electrophoresis and free light chains (to exclude myeloma), testosterone in men, 24-hour urinary calcium, and tissue transglutaneous IgA in selected patients. Bone-turnover markers (CTX, P1NP) help monitor treatment response in selected patients. Trabecular bone score (TBS) on lumbar DEXA, quantitative CT, and high-resolution peripheral CT add micro-architectural information in research and complex cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) of hip and lumbar spine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures areal bone mineral density; T-score at or below -2.5 defines osteoporosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minutes lying still on a padded table. Radiation dose is less than a transatlantic flight. Painless and outpatient. Results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Vertebral fracture assessment (VFA) or lateral thoracolumbar X-ray
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies asymptomatic vertebral compression fractures missed on history
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standing or supine lateral spine images. Adds 2-5 minutes to the DEXA visit. Fractures graded by Genant semi-quantitative method.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, phosphate, PTH, creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for vitamin D deficiency, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and renal contribution
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Most labs return results within 48-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum and urine protein electrophoresis, free light chains
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes monoclonal gammopathy and multiple myeloma in adults over 60 with unexplained fragility fracture or vertebral collapse
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw and a 24-hour urine collection. Results within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Trabecular bone score (TBS) on lumbar DEXA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Adjunctive measure of trabecular microarchitecture; predicts fracture independent of BMD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Automatic post-processing of the lumbar DEXA scan. No extra patient time. Reported as degraded (<1.230), partially degraded (1.230-1.310), or normal (>1.310).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitor response to therapy and adherence
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting morning blood draw. CTX falls by 50% within 3 months of effective bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy. Results within 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: FRAX or Garvan 10-year fracture risk calculator
diagnosticTests.purpose: Estimates 10-year hip and major osteoporotic fracture probability for treatment decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Online or app-based calculator entering age, sex, BMD, prior fracture, parental hip fracture, smoking, alcohol, glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, secondary osteoporosis. Result in seconds.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines anti-resorptive or anabolic pharmacotherapy with calcium, vitamin D, fall prevention, and rehabilitation. The American Association of Clinical Endocrinology 2020, Endocrine Society 2019, and the 2023 update of NOGG (UK) guidance recommend treating any postmenopausal woman or man over 70 with a T-score at or below -2.5, any fragility fracture regardless of T-score, or a 10-year major osteoporotic fracture risk above country-specific FRAX thresholds (often 20% major or 3% hip). First-line therapy for most patients is an oral bisphosphonate — alendronate 70 mg weekly or risedronate 35 mg weekly — taken on an empty stomach with a full glass of water and remaining upright for 30 minutes. Intravenous zoledronic acid 5 mg once yearly is preferred in patients with esophageal disease, poor adherence, or those starting immediately after hip fracture (FRAX Recurrence Trial: 35% reduction in clinical fractures and 28% reduction in all-cause mortality). Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneous every 6 months is a non-bisphosphonate option in patients with renal impairment; abrupt discontinuation causes rapid rebound vertebral fractures, so therapy must be followed by a bisphosphonate. For very-high-risk patients (T-score below -3.0, multiple vertebral fractures, recent hip fracture, or imminent fracture risk), an anabolic agent — teriparatide 20 mcg daily for up to 24 months, abaloparatide, or romosozumab 210 mg monthly for 12 months — is started first, followed by anti-resorptive therapy to consolidate gains. Calcium 1,000-1,200 mg/day (preferably from diet) plus vitamin D3 800-2,000 IU/day to keep 25-hydroxyvitamin D above 30 ng/mL underpin every regimen. Fall-prevention programs, home-hazard removal, footwear correction, vision optimization, and de-prescribing of sedating drugs reduce fracture risk by another 20-30%.

medicalTreatments.name: Alendronate (70 mg oral weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Aminobisphosphonate that binds bone surfaces and induces osteoclast apoptosis. Taken on an empty stomach with water, patient upright for 30 minutes. Common side-effects: dyspepsia, esophagitis, musculoskeletal pain. Continued 5 years then reassessed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture risk by ~50% and hip fracture risk by ~40% over 3-5 years (FIT trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Risedronate (35 mg oral weekly or 150 mg monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative oral bisphosphonate with faster pharmacological recovery. Used in patients with stable creatinine clearance above 30 mL/min.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture risk by ~40% and hip fracture risk by ~30% (VERT, HIP trials)

medicalTreatments.name: Zoledronic acid (5 mg IV once yearly)
medicalTreatments.description: Most potent bisphosphonate, given over 15-30 minutes. Preferred after a recent hip fracture and in patients with poor oral adherence or esophageal disease. Pre-medicate with acetaminophen to reduce post-infusion flu-like reaction. Avoid in eGFR < 35 mL/min.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture risk by 70%, hip fracture by 41%, and all-cause mortality by 28% after hip fracture (HORIZON-PFT, HORIZON-RFT)

medicalTreatments.name: Denosumab (60 mg subcutaneous every 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody to RANKL. Safe in renal impairment. Bone loss rebounds within 7-12 months of discontinuation, with multiple vertebral fractures reported — always followed by a bisphosphonate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture by 68%, hip fracture by 40%, non-vertebral fracture by 20% (FREEDOM trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Teriparatide or abaloparatide (20-80 mcg subcutaneous daily, up to 24 months)
medicalTreatments.description: PTH analogues stimulate bone formation. Used in severe osteoporosis (T-score below -3.0, multiple vertebral fractures, glucocorticoid-induced) followed by anti-resorptive therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture risk by 65-86% and non-vertebral fracture by 35-43% (Fracture Prevention Trial; ACTIVE trial for abaloparatide)

medicalTreatments.name: Romosozumab (210 mg subcutaneous monthly for 12 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-sclerostin monoclonal antibody with dual anabolic and anti-resorptive effect. Avoided in patients with recent stroke or myocardial infarction. Followed by alendronate or denosumab.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral fracture by 73% versus placebo (FRAME) and 50% versus alendronate (ARCH); larger BMD gains than any other agent in first year

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium 1,000-1,200 mg/day total (preferably dietary) and vitamin D3 800-2,000 IU/day. Underpins every pharmacotherapy. Targets serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D above 30 ng/mL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest 10-15% reduction in non-vertebral and hip fractures in institutionalized elders (RECORD, Decalyos trials)

surgicalOptions.name: Hip fracture surgery (cephalomedullary nail, dynamic hip screw, or hemiarthroplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed within 24-48 hours of hip fracture diagnosis. Choice depends on fracture location (intracapsular vs intertrochanteric) and patient functional status.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative mortality 4-8%; one-year functional return to pre-fracture mobility in 40-60%; reduced 30-day mortality with surgery within 24 hours (Hip Fracture Audit data)

surgicalOptions.name: Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous cement injection into a painful collapsed vertebra under fluoroscopic guidance. Considered for refractory pain after 4-6 weeks of conservative care or in selected acute fractures with marked deformity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 60-85% of patients in observational series; randomized trials show benefit in selected acute fractures (VAPOUR trial) and uncertain benefit in chronic pain

surgicalOptions.name: Open reduction and internal fixation of fragility fractures (wrist, humerus, pelvis)
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for displaced or unstable fractures. Surgery uses techniques modified for low bone quality, including locking plates and augmentation with cement.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Bony union in 85-95% of distal radius fractures; functional outcomes depend on rehabilitation and coexisting cognitive status

alternativeTreatments.name: Progressive resistance exercise and weight-bearing physical activity
alternativeTreatments.description: Twice-weekly resistance training and 30 minutes of weight-bearing aerobic activity raise hip and spine BMD by 1-3% and reduce fall rate by 20-30% in adults over 70 (Cochrane 2019, LIFTMOR trial in postmenopausal women).

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi and balance training
alternativeTreatments.description: Tai chi programs (Sun-style or Yang-style) reduce falls by ~30% and improve balance and functional reach. Recommended by AGS-BGS guidelines as a first-line fall-prevention intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a geriatrician, endocrinologist, or rheumatologist when fractures occur on therapy, when secondary causes are suspected (unexplained hypercalcemia, abnormal protein electrophoresis, very-young-age low BMD), when severe T-scores below -3.0 require anabolic therapy, or when complex polypharmacy and frailty complicate management. Patients with hip fracture should be enrolled in a fracture liaison service for systematic post-fracture bone-protective treatment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician affiliated with a fracture liaison service or osteoporosis clinic. Ask whether they routinely use FRAX, perform vertebral fracture assessment, prescribe parenteral therapy (zoledronate, denosumab, romosozumab), and coordinate with falls and rehabilitation teams. Continuity over years matters because bisphosphonate drug holidays and post-denosumab transitions require ongoing oversight.
hospitalCriteria: On-site DEXA service with vertebral fracture assessment capability
Fracture liaison service or osteoporosis clinic with multidisciplinary input
Orthogeriatric co-management for hip fracture patients
Access to parenteral therapy (zoledronate, denosumab, teriparatide, romosozumab)
Falls clinic and home-safety assessment program
keyFacilities: Endocrinology and metabolic bone disease clinic
Orthopedic trauma service with hip fracture pathway
preventionStrategies: Maintain dietary calcium intake of 1,000-1,200 mg/day from dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, sardines, and tofu.
Achieve serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D above 30 ng/mL with 800-2,000 IU/day vitamin D3, especially during winter and in housebound elders.
Perform progressive resistance training twice weekly and 30 minutes of weight-bearing aerobic activity most days.
Quit smoking and limit alcohol to less than 2 units/day to slow BMD decline and reduce falls.
Review medications annually with a pharmacist to limit chronic glucocorticoid use, deprescribe sedating drugs, and use the lowest effective doses of PPIs and SSRIs.
Optimize home safety: remove loose rugs, install grab bars in bathrooms, ensure adequate lighting, and use stair handrails to prevent falls.
Get annual vision and hearing checks; treat cataracts and provide appropriate eyewear since visual deficits triple fall risk.
lifestyleModifications: Take oral bisphosphonates first thing in the morning with 240 mL plain water; remain upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes; avoid food and other medications for that time.
Use a calcium-rich diet first; reserve calcium supplements (under 500 mg/dose) for those who cannot reach 1,000 mg/day from food, to limit cardiovascular and stone-formation concerns.
Practice balance exercises (tai chi, single-leg stance with support) for 10-15 minutes daily to reduce fall risk.
Wear well-fitted, low-heeled, non-slip footwear indoors and outdoors; avoid walking in socks alone on hard floors.
Use hip protectors in high-risk frail residents — meta-analyses show 20-30% reduction in hip fracture in nursing homes when adherence is good.
Maintain protein intake of 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day to support muscle mass and bone matrix synthesis.
Have an annual dental review; bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw is rare (under 0.05% in osteoporosis dosing) but extractions during therapy require coordination.

recommended: Calcium-rich foods: dairy products, fortified plant-based milks, sardines, canned salmon with bones, tofu set with calcium sulfate, kale, broccoli
Vitamin D sources: fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, herring), egg yolks, fortified milk and cereals, modest sun exposure
Protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day from fish, poultry, legumes, eggs, dairy
Vitamin K2 from fermented foods (natto), cheese, egg yolk for bone-matrix carboxylation
Magnesium and zinc-rich foods: nuts, seeds, whole grains, legumes
Mediterranean-style diet pattern, associated with 20-30% lower hip-fracture risk in cohort studies
avoid: Excessive sodium (above 2,300 mg/day) which increases urinary calcium loss
More than three caffeinated drinks daily (above 400 mg caffeine) without adequate calcium intake
More than 2 units of alcohol per day
Carbonated soft drinks high in phosphoric acid as the main beverage
High-dose vitamin A supplements (above 3,000 mcg retinol/day) which raise hip fracture risk in observational data

exerciseGuidelines: Combine progressive resistance training (2-3 sessions/week working all major muscle groups at 60-80% of one-repetition maximum), weight-bearing aerobic activity (30 minutes most days such as brisk walking or stair climbing), and balance/proprioceptive work (tai chi, single-leg stance with support, heel-to-toe walking). Patients with vertebral fractures should avoid heavy forward-flexion exercises (sit-ups, toe-touches) and instead emphasize spine-extension exercises (prone press-ups, scapular retraction). Supervised programs delivered by a physiotherapist trained in osteoporosis achieve the largest BMD and fall-prevention gains.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed bisphosphonate or denosumab exactly on schedule; missed doses of denosumab beyond one month risk rebound vertebral fractures.
Take a daily vitamin D3 supplement at 800-2,000 IU with a meal containing fat to maximize absorption.
Spread calcium intake across meals; doses above 500-600 mg at once exceed intestinal absorption capacity.
Perform 10-15 minutes of balance and lower-limb strengthening daily plus 2 longer resistance sessions weekly.
Keep a current medication list and discuss any new drug with the prescriber for bone-mass implications.
Wear non-slip footwear and use mobility aids if recommended; check home lighting nightly.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on age at diagnosis, BMD severity, fracture history, comorbidities, and adherence to therapy. Patients with osteoporosis treated for 3-5 years with bisphosphonates achieve 30-50% reductions in hip and vertebral fracture risk and roughly 5-7% gain in lumbar BMD. Patients who sustain a hip fracture face a 1-year mortality of 20-30% (40% in men) and only 30-50% recover their pre-fracture level of independent walking; one in four requires long-term institutional care. Vertebral fractures carry a 5-year mortality of 15-20% and a 5-fold risk of a second vertebral fracture within 1 year if untreated. Anabolic therapy followed by an antiresorptive (treat-to-target strategy) raises BMD into the osteopenic range within 18-24 months in roughly 50% of severe patients. Mortality benefit is documented for zoledronic acid initiated after hip fracture (HORIZON-RFT: 28% reduction at 1.9 years) and is plausible for denosumab and oral bisphosphonates in elders with high comorbidity.
recoveryTimeline: BMD response to bisphosphonates is detectable on DEXA at 12-24 months; anabolic agents produce visible spine BMD gains within 6-12 months. Bone-turnover markers (CTX, P1NP) fall by 30-70% within 3 months of effective anti-resorptive therapy. Acute vertebral fracture pain settles in 4-12 weeks; chronic kyphosis-related pain may persist. Functional recovery after hip fracture: 40-60% of patients return to pre-fracture walking by 6 months; full recovery extends to 12 months in best cases.
complications: Hip fracture with associated pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis, pressure injury, and delirium; one-year mortality 20-30% (40% in men). Mitigate with prompt surgery, multimodal analgesia, and orthogeriatric care.
Vertebral compression fractures producing chronic pain, kyphosis, height loss, and restrictive lung disease. Early DEXA-VFA in painful back episodes identifies these.
Osteoporotic pelvic and sacral insufficiency fractures, often missed on plain radiographs; require MRI or CT.
Falls cycle: post-fracture deconditioning leads to further falls and refractures within 12-24 months; structured fall-prevention programs reduce this by 20-30%.
Atypical femoral fractures (subtrochanteric, transverse) after 5+ years of bisphosphonate therapy; rare (1-2 per 10,000 person-years) but recognizable by prodromal thigh pain.
Bisphosphonate- or denosumab-related osteonecrosis of the jaw, especially after invasive dental work; incidence under 0.1% at osteoporosis doses but warrants dental optimization before therapy.

supportResources.name: Bone Health & Osteoporosis Foundation (BHOF, US)
supportResources.description: Patient education, FRAX guidance, and clinician finder for the United States.

supportResources.name: International Osteoporosis Foundation (IOF)
supportResources.description: Global advocacy, World Osteoporosis Day resources, and patient guides in multiple languages.

supportResources.name: Royal Osteoporosis Society (UK)
supportResources.description: UK-based patient charity with helpline, exercise resources, and post-fracture support.

supportResources.name: American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria & Falls Guidance
supportResources.description: Authoritative guidance on medications to avoid in older adults and the AGS-BGS Falls Prevention Clinical Practice Guideline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Postmenopausal Osteoporosis (Type I)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type I postmenopausal osteoporosis affects women within 5-10 years of menopause, predominantly causes trabecular bone loss with vertebral and distal radius fractures, and is estrogen-deficiency-driven. Senile (Type II) osteoporosis occurs after 70, affects both cortical and trabecular bone, presents with hip and pelvic fractures, and is driven by calcium-vitamin D-PTH axis failure plus reduced osteoblast activity.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcopenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcopenia is age-related loss of muscle mass and strength diagnosed by grip strength, gait speed, and DEXA fat-free mass. Senile osteoporosis is bone loss diagnosed by DEXA BMD. The two often coexist as osteosarcopenia and together drive falls and fractures, but the diagnostic tests and primary treatments differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bone Metastatic Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metastatic cancer (lung, breast, prostate, kidney, multiple myeloma) can present with pathological fractures and vertebral collapse mimicking osteoporosis. Pedicle destruction, lytic or sclerotic lesions on imaging, hypercalcemia, monoclonal gammopathy, and constitutional symptoms point to malignancy. MRI and biopsy distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frailty is a clinical syndrome of decreased physiological reserve identified by weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slow walking speed, and low activity (Fried criteria). Senile osteoporosis is a bone-density diagnosis. The two overlap heavily; frailty raises fracture risk and complicates osteoporosis treatment but is itself reversible with resistance training and nutrition.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The canonical osteoporosis condition page; senile osteoporosis is the age-related subtype.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcopenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists as osteosarcopenia and shares treatment overlap with resistance exercise and protein intake.

relatedConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Major risk multiplier for fragility fractures and post-fracture mortality in adults over 75.

relatedConditions.name: Falls in Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Mechanism for the majority of hip and wrist fractures; fall prevention is integral to fracture prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Common reversible cause of secondary osteoporosis; checked on every work-up.

faqs.question: What is senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Senile osteoporosis is the age-related form of bone loss affecting adults over 70 of both sexes. It is driven by chronic vitamin D and calcium deficiency, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and reduced osteoblast activity, and produces hip, vertebral, and pelvic fractures after minimal trauma.

faqs.question: How is senile osteoporosis different from postmenopausal osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Postmenopausal (Type I) osteoporosis affects women in the first decade after menopause, mainly erodes trabecular bone, and produces vertebral and wrist fractures. Senile (Type II) osteoporosis occurs after age 70, affects both cortical and trabecular bone, and produces hip and pelvic fractures driven by calcium-vitamin D axis failure rather than estrogen loss.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Most patients have no symptoms before fracture. Warning signs include loss of 2 cm or more in height, new mid-back pain, progressive stooped posture, a first fragility fracture after minimal trauma (such as a wrist fracture from a fall on an outstretched hand), and difficulty rising from a chair without arms.

faqs.question: How is senile osteoporosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a fragility fracture history and DEXA scan of the hip and spine. A T-score at or below -2.5 defines osteoporosis. Any fragility fracture in an adult over 50 qualifies as clinical osteoporosis regardless of T-score. Blood tests exclude secondary causes such as vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, or myeloma.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: First-line therapy is an oral bisphosphonate (alendronate 70 mg weekly or risedronate 35 mg weekly) or intravenous zoledronic acid 5 mg yearly. Denosumab is preferred when kidney function is reduced. Severe disease starts with an anabolic agent (teriparatide or romosozumab) followed by an antiresorptive. Calcium, vitamin D, and fall prevention support every regimen.

faqs.question: How much calcium and vitamin D do older adults need?
faqs.answer: Adults over 70 need 1,000-1,200 mg of calcium and 800-2,000 IU of vitamin D3 daily, preferably from diet plus supplements as needed. The goal is serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D above 30 ng/mL. Calcium doses above 500-600 mg at once exceed intestinal absorption capacity, so spread intake across meals.

faqs.question: Can men get senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 6-10% of men age 75 and over have osteoporosis by DEXA criteria. One in five men over 50 will sustain a fragility fracture. One-year mortality after hip fracture is 30-40% in men, higher than in women. Men remain under-screened and under-treated despite established treatments being equally effective.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate after a hip fracture in the elderly?
faqs.answer: One-year mortality after hip fracture is 20-30% overall and 30-40% in men. Roughly 40-60% of survivors recover their pre-fracture walking ability. About 25% require long-term institutional care. Surgery within 24-48 hours, orthogeriatric co-management, and starting zoledronic acid before discharge lower mortality.

faqs.question: Can senile osteoporosis be reversed?
faqs.answer: Bone density gains of 5-10% over 2-3 years are achievable with anabolic agents (teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab) followed by anti-resorptive therapy. Bisphosphonates produce smaller gains (2-5%) but still cut hip fracture risk by 30-50%. Severe established osteoporosis is rarely returned to a fully normal T-score, but fracture risk falls dramatically.

faqs.question: How long do you take bisphosphonates?
faqs.answer: Typical oral bisphosphonate courses last 5 years, after which low-risk patients may take a drug holiday under monitoring. Patients with persistent T-scores below -2.5 or recent fractures continue therapy for up to 10 years. Intravenous zoledronate is often given for 3 years before reassessment.

faqs.question: What are the side effects of osteoporosis medications?
faqs.answer: Oral bisphosphonates can cause heartburn, esophagitis, and musculoskeletal pain. Zoledronic acid causes a flu-like reaction in 30% of first doses. Rare risks include atypical femur fracture (1-2 per 10,000 person-years after 5+ years) and osteonecrosis of the jaw (under 0.1% at osteoporosis doses). Denosumab discontinuation can cause rebound vertebral fractures.

faqs.question: How can falls be prevented in older adults with osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Effective measures include twice-weekly resistance and balance training (tai chi reduces falls by ~30%), home hazard removal (loose rugs, poor lighting), annual vision checks, treating cataracts, deprescribing sedating medications, well-fitted non-slip footwear, and vitamin D 800-1,000 IU/day in those with low levels.

faqs.question: Should I take calcium supplements?
faqs.answer: Aim to meet 1,000-1,200 mg/day from diet first. Calcium supplements are added only if dietary intake falls short. Use elemental calcium doses of 500 mg or less per serving to maximize absorption. Some evidence links high-dose calcium supplements (above 1,000 mg/day) to cardiovascular events, so dietary calcium is preferred.

faqs.question: What exercises are safe with senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Progressive resistance training, weight-bearing aerobic activity (walking, stair climbing), tai chi, and supervised balance work are safe and effective. Avoid heavy forward-bending exercises (sit-ups, toe-touches) if vertebral fractures are present; these can cause further compression. A physiotherapist trained in osteoporosis can tailor a program.

faqs.question: What is osteosarcopenia?
faqs.answer: Osteosarcopenia is the combination of osteoporosis (low bone mass) and sarcopenia (low muscle mass and strength). Together they multiply fall and fracture risk and predict worse recovery after hip fracture. Treatment combines bisphosphonates or denosumab for bone with resistance exercise and protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day for muscle.

faqs.question: Can senile osteoporosis cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. New severe mid-back or low-back pain after minor exertion may indicate a vertebral compression fracture. Pain typically peaks for 4-6 weeks then settles into a chronic ache. Multiple vertebral fractures produce kyphosis, restrictive lung function, and reduced quality of life.

faqs.question: Do you need a DEXA scan every year?
faqs.answer: Repeat DEXA is generally not recommended yearly because real BMD change takes 18-24 months to detect reliably. Most guidelines suggest repeat DEXA every 1-2 years on therapy and every 2-5 years off therapy, individualized by baseline risk. Vertebral fracture imaging is added when height loss or new back pain occurs.

faqs.question: Can senile osteoporosis affect teeth and dental work?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mandibular bone loss accompanies systemic osteoporosis and contributes to tooth loosening and tooth loss. Bisphosphonates and denosumab can rarely cause osteonecrosis of the jaw after extractions or implant surgery (under 0.1% at osteoporosis doses), so significant dental work is best completed before starting therapy.

faqs.question: Is osteoporosis screening recommended for everyone over 70?
faqs.answer: The US Preventive Services Task Force recommends DEXA for all women age 65 and older and for postmenopausal women under 65 with elevated risk by FRAX. For men, evidence is less robust; many guidelines recommend screening men over 70, especially with hip-fracture risk factors or prior fragility fracture.

faqs.question: What is a fracture liaison service?
faqs.answer: A fracture liaison service is a coordinated program that identifies adults over 50 who sustain a fragility fracture, assesses bone density and fracture risk, prescribes anti-osteoporosis therapy, arranges follow-up, and integrates falls prevention. Fracture liaison services cut refracture rates by 30-40% and are recommended in international guidelines.

faqs.question: Can diet alone prevent senile osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Diet alone cannot reverse established senile osteoporosis but contributes substantially to prevention. Adequate calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day), vitamin D (800-2,000 IU/day), protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day), and a Mediterranean-style pattern are linked to 20-30% lower hip-fracture risk. Resistance exercise and pharmacotherapy are needed when bone density is already low.

simpleName: Senile Osteoporosis (age-related Type II bone loss)
metaTitle: Senile Osteoporosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Senile osteoporosis: age-related bone loss after 70 causing hip and spine fractures. Symptoms, DEXA diagnosis, bisphosphonate and anabolic treatment.
